preamble: The shift from the postalveolar fricative [ʃ] to the high-front vowel [i] (or palatal glide [j]) is a form of vocalization. Because the articulation of [ʃ] involves the tongue blade and front body being raised toward the palate, it is phonetically adjacent to the high front vowel [i]. When friction is lost, the vocalic quality remains.

examples.note: Vocalization of part of the sibilant's duration into a palatal glide, creating a diphthong.
The palatal/postalveolar fricative vocalized into the high front element of the modern English diphthong [aɪ].

sources: Baugh & Cable: A History of the English Language
